prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response:
Henryk Górecki – Symphony No. 3, 2nd movement (“Lento e largo”).  
A fifteen-year-old girl’s prayer, scratched on the wall of a Gestapo cell, is sung slowly, over and over, by a soprano who sounds as if she’s already become a memory. The orchestra does almost nothing—just three chords breathing in and out—yet each time the voice returns it carries a little more of the listener’s own life with it, until the room you’re sitting in feels suddenly cavernous and crowded with every person you failed to save. The piece doesn’t ask you to feel sorry for the dead; it asks you to admit that you’re still alive, and that this is an unbearable, luminous fact.
